In this work, we evaluate three strong segmentation architectures (UNet, DeepLabV3 with a ResNet50 backbone, and DINOv2) on two public dermoscopic datasets (HAM10000 and ISIC2017) and introduce a continuous pigment or contrast analysis that treats pixel-wise ITA values as distributions. Using Wasserstein distances between within-image distributions for skin-only, lesion-only, and whole-image regions, we quantify lesion skin contrast and relate it to segmentation performance across multiple metrics. Within the range represented in these datasets, global skin tone metrics (Fitzpatrick grouping or mean ITA) show weak association with segmentation quality. In contrast, low lesion-skin contrast is consistently associated with larger segmentation errors in models, indicating that boundary ambiguity and low contrast are key drivers of failure. These findings suggest that fairness improvements in dermoscopic segmentation should prioritize robust handling of low-contrast lesions, and the distribution-based pigment measures provide a more informative audit signal than discrete skin-tone categories.

AI.tldr: This paper explores the impact of skin tone and lesion-skin contrast on AI-driven skin lesion segmentation performance, proposing a continuous pigment analysis over discrete categories to enhance fairness and segmentation accuracy.
AI.motivation: Skin cancer remains a significant health issue, and fair, accurate AI systems for skin lesion segmentation are crucial for early detection. However, the role of skin tone, particularly lesion-skin contrast, in segmentation performance has been underexplored.
AI.method: The researchers evaluated three segmentation models (UNet, DeepLabV3 with ResNet50 backbone, DINOv2) on two public datasets (HAM10000, ISIC2017) using a novel analysis that leverages pixel-wise ITA values and Wasserstein distances to assess lesion-skin contrast and its impact on segmentation accuracy.
AI.result: The study found that global skin tone metrics (such as Fitzpatrick grouping or mean ITA) have weak association with segmentation quality, while low lesion-skin contrast is consistently linked with higher segmentation errors.
AI.conclusion: Fairness-focused improvements in dermoscopic segmentation should target better handling of low-contrast lesions, and distribution-based pigment measures provide more nuanced insights than discrete skin tone categories.

title: Minimum Norm Interpolation via The Local Theory of Banach Spaces: The Role of $2$-Uniform Convexity
comment: A Preliminary work of this work "Minimum Norm Interpolation Meets The Local Theory of Banach Spaces'' appeared at the International Conference of Machine Learning 2024 (consider this info for citations)
summary: The minimum-norm interpolator (MNI) framework has recently attracted considerable attention as a tool for understanding generalization in overparameterized models, such as neural networks. In this work, we study the MNI under a $2$-uniform convexity assumption, which is weaker than requiring the norm to be induced by an inner product, and it typically does not admit a closed-form solution. At a high level, we show that this condition yields an upper bound on the MNI bias in both linear and nonlinear models. We further show that this bound is sharp for overparameterized linear regression when the unit ball of the norm is in isotropic (or John's) position, and the covariates are isotropic, symmetric, i.i.d. sub-Gaussian, such as vectors with i.i.d. Bernoulli entries. Finally, under the same assumption on the covariates, we prove sharp generalization bounds for the $\ell_p$-MNI when $p \in \bigl(1 + C/\log d, 2\bigr]$. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to establish sharp bounds for non-Gaussian covariates in linear models when the norm is not induced by an inner product. This work is deeply inspired by classical works on $K$-convexity, and more modern work on the geometry of 2-uniform and isotropic convex bodies.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes the minimum-norm interpolator (MNI) under weaker convexity assumptions, establishing sharp bias and generalization bounds for various setups.
AI.motivation: The motivation stems from the need to understand generalization properties in overparameterized models like neural networks, specifically through the lens of the minimum-norm interpolator (MNI).
AI.method: The authors assume a weaker 2-uniform convexity framework, study its implications on MNI bias, and derive sharp bounds for linear regression and generalization under specific conditions.
AI.result: The study provides sharp bias bounds for overparameterized linear regression under isotropic conditions, and sharp generalization bounds for the $\ell_p$-MNI when $p \in (1 + C/\log d, 2]$.
AI.conclusion: The findings extend understanding of MNI generalization and bias to setups beyond Gaussian covariates or norms induced by inner products, using concepts from convexity and geometry.

title: FED-Bench: A Cross-Granular Benchmark for Disentangled Evaluation of Facial Expression Editing
summary: Facial expression image editing requires fine-grained control to strictly preserve human identity and background while precisely manipulating expression. However, existing editing benchmarks primarily focus on general scenarios, lacking high-quality facial images and corresponding editing instructions. Furthermore, current evaluation metrics exhibit systemic biases in this task, often favoring lazy editing or overfit editing. To bridge these gaps, we propose FED-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark featuring rigorous testing and an accurate evaluation suite. First, we carefully construct a benchmark of 747 triplets through a cascaded and scalable pipeline, each comprising an original image, an editing instruction, and a ground-truth image for precise evaluation. Second, we introduce FED-Score, a cross-granularity evaluation protocol that disentangles assessment into three dimensions: Alignment for verifying instruction following, Fidelity for testing image quality and identity preservation, and Relative Expression Gain for quantifying the magnitude of expression changes, effectively mitigating the aforementioned evaluation biases. Third, we benchmark 18 image editing models, revealing that current approaches struggle to simultaneously achieve high fidelity and accurate expression manipulation, with fine-grained instruction following identified as the primary bottleneck. Finally, leveraging the scalable characteristic of introduced benchmark engine, we provide a 20k+ in-the-wild facial training set and demonstrate its effectiveness by fine-tuning a baseline model that achieves significant performance gains. Our benchmark and related code will be made publicly open soon.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a new benchmark, FED-Bench, designed for high-quality evaluation of facial expression image editing with fine-grained control. It identifies shortcomings in current models and evaluation metrics, offering new datasets and methods for rigorous testing.
AI.motivation: Current benchmarks and metrics for facial expression editing lack focus on high-quality images, precise editing instructions, and unbiased evaluation methods, which hinders progress in achieving accurate and identity-preserving image manipulation.
AI.method: The authors construct FED-Bench with 747 annotated triplets and design a new evaluation protocol called FED-Score, which assesses alignment, fidelity, and relative expression gain. They also benchmark 18 models and introduce a large-scale training dataset.
AI.result: The analysis reveals that existing models struggle with fine-grained expression editing while maintaining high fidelity. Fine-tuning with the new dataset leads to significant improvements in model performance.
AI.conclusion: FED-Bench offers a high-quality benchmark and metrics, addressing critical gaps in the facial expression editing domain. It enables more rigorous development and evaluation of models, with the tools and data made publicly available.

title: Privacy Guard & Token Parsimony by Prompt and Context Handling and LLM Routing
summary: The large-scale adoption of Large Language Models (LLMs) forces a trade-off between operational cost (OpEx) and data privacy. Current routing frameworks reduce costs but ignore prompt sensitivity, exposing users and institutions to leakage risks towards third-party cloud providers. We formalise the "Inseparability Paradigm": advanced context management intrinsically coincides with privacy management. We propose a local "Privacy Guard" -- a holistic contextual observer powered by an on-premise Small Language Model (SLM) -- that performs abstractive summarisation and Automatic Prompt Optimisation (APO) to decompose prompts into focused sub-tasks, re-routing high-risk queries to Zero-Trust or NDA-covered models. This dual mechanism simultaneously eliminates sensitive inference vectors (Zero Leakage) and reduces cloud token payloads (OpEx Reduction). A LIFO-based context compacting mechanism further bounds working memory, limiting the emergent leakage surface. We validate the framework through a 2x2 benchmark (Lazy vs. Expert users; Personal vs. Institutional secrets) on a 1,000-sample dataset, achieving a 45% blended OpEx reduction, 100% redaction success on personal secrets, and -- via LLM-as-a-Judge evaluation -- an 85% preference rate for APO-compressed responses over raw baselines. Our results demonstrate that Token Parsimony and Zero Leakage are mathematically dual projections of the same contextual compression operator.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a framework using Small Language Models (SLMs) for privacy-focused prompt optimization and operational cost reduction in Large Language Models (LLMs).
AI.motivation: The need to balance operational costs and data privacy in the widespread use of Large Language Models (LLMs).
AI.method: An on-premise Privacy Guard powered by Small Language Models (SLMs) that performs abstractive summarisation, Automatic Prompt Optimisation (APO), query decomposition, and re-routing to secure or zero-trust models.
AI.result: The framework achieved 45% cost reduction, 100% redaction success on personal secrets, and high user preference rates (85%) for optimized responses versus baselines.
AI.conclusion: Token Parsimony and Zero Leakage are mathematically intertwined through contextual compression operators, empowering privacy and cost efficiency simultaneously.

title: Compressive sensing inspired self-supervised single-pixel imaging
comment: 10 pages, 9 figures, 2 algorithms, 2 tables, journal paper
summary: Single-pixel imaging (SPI) is a promising imaging modality with distinctive advantages in strongly perturbed environments. Existing SPI methods lack physical sparsity constraints and overlook the integration of local and global features, leading to severe noise vulnerability, structural distortions and blurred details. To address these limitations, we propose SISTA-Net, a compressive sensing-inspired self-supervised method for single-pixel imaging. SISTA-Net unfolds the Iterative Shrinkage-Thresholding Algorithm (ISTA) into an interpretable network consisting of a data fidelity module and a proximal mapping module. The fidelity module adopts a hybrid CNN-Visual State Space Model (VSSM) architecture to integrate local and global feature modeling, enhancing reconstruction integrity and fidelity. We leverage deep nonlinear networks as adaptive sparse transforms combined with a learnable soft-thresholding operator to impose explicit physical sparsity in the latent domain, enabling noise suppression and robustness to interference even at extremely low sampling rates. Extensive experiments on multiple simulation scenarios demonstrate that SISTA-Net outperforms state-of-the-art methods by 2.6 dB in PSNR. Real-world far-field underwater tests yield a 3.4 dB average PSNR improvement, validating its robust anti-interference capability.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SISTA-Net, a new self-supervised method for single-pixel imaging (SPI) that enhances noise robustness, fidelity, and sparsity using an interpretable iterative algorithm coupled with advanced CNNs and adaptive deep sparse transforms.
AI.motivation: To overcome limitations in existing SPI methods, such as lack of physical sparsity constraints and inadequate feature integration causing noise vulnerability and structural distortions.
AI.method: SISTA-Net uses the Iterative Shrinkage-Thresholding Algorithm (ISTA) in a hybrid CNN-Visual State Space Model architecture to model local and global features, combined with adaptive sparse transforms and learnable soft-thresholding for explicit sparsity.
AI.result: The approach yields improvements up to 2.6 dB PSNR in simulations and 3.4 dB average PSNR in real-world tests, showcasing enhanced reconstruction and interference suppression.
AI.conclusion: SISTA-Net proves effective in addressing SPI's challenges, providing superior fidelity, noise resistance, and robustness at low sampling rates, outperforming state-of-the-art methods.

title: Design Principles for the Construction of a Benchmark Evaluating Security Operation Capabilities of Multi-agent AI Systems
summary: As Large Language Models (LLMs) and multi-agent AI systems are demonstrating increasing potential in cybersecurity operations, organizations, policymakers, model providers, and researchers in the AI and cybersecurity communities are interested in quantifying the capabilities of such AI systems to achieve more autonomous SOCs (security operation centers) and reduce manual effort. In particular, the AI and cybersecurity communities have recently developed several benchmarks for evaluating the red team capabilities of multi-agent AI systems. However, because the operations in SOCs are dominated by blue team operations, the capabilities of AI systems & agents to achieve more autonomous SOCs cannot be evaluated without a benchmark focused on blue team operations. To our best knowledge, no systematic benchmark for evaluating coordinated multi-task blue team AI has been proposed in the literature. Existing blue team benchmarks focus on a particular task. The goal of this work is to develop a set of design principles for the construction of a benchmark, which is denoted as SOC-bench, to evaluate the blue team capabilities of AI. Following these design principles, we have developed a conceptual design of SOC-bench, which consists of a family of five blue team tasks in the context of large-scale ransomware attack incident response.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses the lack of benchmarks for evaluating AI's blue team capabilities in cybersecurity by proposing SOC-bench.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to create a systematic benchmark for evaluating AI systems' effectiveness in blue team cybersecurity operations, as current benchmarks focus primarily on red team capabilities or single tasks.
AI.method: The authors propose design principles for SOC-bench, a benchmark that evaluates capabilities across five blue team tasks in ransomware incident response.
AI.result: A conceptual design of SOC-bench was developed to assess multi-task blue team AI capabilities.
AI.conclusion: SOC-bench fills a gap in cybersecurity benchmarking by providing a systematic way to evaluate AI's blue team roles and fostering innovation toward autonomous SOCs.

title: A Pontryagin Method of Model-based Reinforcement Learning via Hamiltonian Actor-Critic
summary: Model-based reinforcement learning (MBRL) improves sample efficiency by leveraging learned dynamics models for policy optimization. However, the effectiveness of methods such as actor-critic is often limited by compounding model errors, which degrade long-horizon value estimation. Existing approaches, such as Model-Based Value Expansion (MVE), partially mitigate this issue through multi-step rollouts, but remain sensitive to rollout horizon selection and residual model bias. Motivated by the Pontryagin Maximum Principle (PMP), we propose Hamiltonian Actor-Critic (HAC), a model-based approach that eliminates explicit value function learning by directly optimizing a Hamiltonian defined over the learned dynamics and reward for deterministic systems. By avoiding value approximation, HAC reduces sensitivity to model errors while admitting convergence guarantees. Extensive experiments on continuous control benchmarks, in both online and offline RL settings, demonstrate that HAC outperforms model-free and MVE-based baselines in control performance, convergence speed, and robustness to distributional shift, including out-of-distribution (OOD) scenarios. In offline settings with limited data, HAC matches or exceeds state-of-the-art methods, highlighting its strong sample efficiency.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces "Hamiltonian Actor-Critic" (HAC), a reinforcement learning method that avoids explicit value function learning to reduce sensitivity to model errors, showing superior performance on various benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of model-based reinforcement learning caused by compounding model errors and the challenges of value function approximation in long-horizon tasks.
AI.method: The proposed HAC directly optimizes a Hamiltonian defined over learned dynamics and rewards for deterministic systems, bypassing value estimation and leveraging properties of the Pontryagin Maximum Principle (PMP).
AI.result: Experimental results demonstrate that HAC outperforms both model-free and MVE-based methods in terms of performance, speed, and resilience to distributional shifts, including strong results in offline settings with limited data.
AI.conclusion: HAC provides a robust and efficient alternative to traditional MBRL approaches, offering better sample efficiency and reduced error sensitivity by relying on Hamiltonian optimization instead of value function approximation.

title: Leveraging Synthetic Data for Enhancing Egocentric Hand-Object Interaction Detection
summary: In this work, we explore the role of synthetic data in improving the detection of Hand-Object Interactions from egocentric images. Through extensive experimentation and comparative analysis on VISOR, EgoHOS, and ENIGMA-51 datasets, our findings demonstrate the potential of synthetic data to significantly improve HOI detection, particularly when real labeled data are scarce or unavailable. By using synthetic data and only 10% of the real labeled data, we achieve improvements in Overall AP over models trained exclusively on real data, with gains of +5.67% on VISOR, +8.24% on EgoHOS, and +11.69% on ENIGMA-51. Furthermore, we systematically study how aligning synthetic data to specific real-world benchmarks with respect to objects, grasps, and environments, showing that the effectiveness of synthetic data consistently improves with better synthetic-real alignment. As a result of this work, we release a new data generation pipeline and the new HOI-Synth benchmark, which augments existing datasets with synthetic images of hand-object interaction. These data are automatically annotated with hand-object contact states, bounding boxes, and pixel-wise segmentation masks. All data, code, and tools for synthetic data generation are available at: https://fpv-iplab.github.io/HOI-Synth/.

AI.tldr: This study investigates the impact of synthetic data on Hand-Object Interaction (HOI) detection in egocentric images, showing significant performance improvements across multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of detecting Hand-Object Interactions in scenarios where real labeled data is limited or unavailable.
AI.method: Synthetic data was employed alongside small quantities of real labeled data, with a focus on aligning synthetic data to real-world benchmarks for objects, grasps, and environments.
AI.result: Using synthetic data and only 10% real labeled data, improvements in Overall AP were achieved: +5.67% on VISOR, +8.24% on EgoHOS, and +11.69% on ENIGMA-51.
AI.conclusion: Integrating synthetic data enhances HOI detection, especially with better synthetic-real alignment, and a new data generation pipeline and benchmark were introduced for widespread adoption.

title: SHIFT: Stochastic Hidden-Trajectory Deflection for Removing Diffusion-based Watermark
summary: Diffusion-based watermarking methods embed verifiable marks by manipulating the initial noise or the reverse diffusion trajectory. However, these methods share a critical assumption: verification can succeed only if the diffusion trajectory can be faithfully reconstructed. This reliance on trajectory recovery constitutes a fundamental and exploitable vulnerability. We propose $\underline{\mathbf{S}}$tochastic $\underline{\mathbf{Hi}}$dden-Trajectory De$\underline{\mathbf{f}}$lec$\underline{\mathbf{t}}$ion ($\mathbf{SHIFT}$), a training-free attack that exploits this common weakness across diverse watermarking paradigms. SHIFT leverages stochastic diffusion resampling to deflect the generative trajectory in latent space, making the reconstructed image statistically decoupled from the original watermark-embedded trajectory while preserving strong visual quality and semantic consistency. Extensive experiments on nine representative watermarking methods spanning noise-space, frequency-domain, and optimization-based paradigms show that SHIFT achieves 95%--100% attack success rates with nearly no loss in semantic quality, without requiring any watermark-specific knowledge or model retraining.

AI.tldr: The study presents SHIFT, a training-free attack targeting diffusion-based watermarking methods by deflecting the generative trajectory, achieving high attack success rates without sacrificing image quality.
AI.motivation: To address the exploitable vulnerability in diffusion-based watermarking methods which rely on faithful trajectory recovery for verification.
AI.method: The proposed method, SHIFT, uses stochastic diffusion resampling to decouple reconstructed images from watermark-embedded trajectories, preserving semantic and visual fidelity.
AI.result: SHIFT demonstrates 95%--100% attack success rates across nine watermarking methods while maintaining semantic consistency and requiring neither watermark-specific knowledge nor model retraining.
AI.conclusion: SHIFT reveals a critical weakness in diffusion-based watermarking approaches and provides a highly effective attack mechanism, emphasizing the need for more robust watermarking paradigms.

title: Data-informed lifting line theory
summary: We present a data-driven framework that extends the predictive capability of classical lifting-line theory (LLT) to a wider aerodynamic regime by incorporating higher-fidelity aerodynamic data from panel method simulations. A neural network architecture with a convolutional layer followed by fully connected layers is developed, comprising two parallel subnetworks to separately process spanwise collocation points and global geometric/aerodynamic inputs such as angle of attack, chord, twist, airfoil distribution, and sweep. Among several configurations tested, this architecture is most effective in learning corrections to LLT outputs. The trained model captures higher-order three-dimensional effects in spanwise lift and drag distributions in regimes where LLT is inaccurate, such as low aspect ratios and high sweep, and generalizes well to wing configurations outside both the LLT regime and the training data range. The method retains LLT's computational efficiency, enabling integration into aerodynamic optimization loops and early-stage aircraft design studies. This approach offers a practical path for embedding high-fidelity corrections into low-order methods and may be extended to other aerodynamic prediction tasks, such as propeller performance.

AI.tldr: The paper presents an enhanced lifting-line theory (LLT) integrated with a neural network using panel method data for improved aerodynamic predictions beyond LLT's traditional scope.
AI.motivation: Classical LLT is computationally efficient but limited in accuracy for complex aerodynamics, needing higher-fidelity corrections for broader applicability.
AI.method: The authors introduce a neural network with convolutional and fully connected layers, processing spanwise and global inputs to integrate corrections from panel method simulations to LLT outputs.
AI.result: Their model effectively captures higher-order effects and improves predictions for configurations outside LLT's accuracy range, such as low aspect ratios and high sweep.
AI.conclusion: The framework enhances LLT with computational efficiency intact, making it practical for aerodynamic optimization and early-stage aircraft design, with potential applications in other areas like propeller performance.

title: TSHA: A Benchmark for Visual Language Models in Trustworthy Safety Hazard Assessment Scenarios
summary: Recent advances in vision-language models (VLMs) have accelerated their application to indoor safety hazards assessment. However, existing benchmarks suffer from three fundamental limitations: (1) heavy reliance on synthetic datasets constructed via simulation software, creating a significant domain gap with real-world environments; (2) oversimplified safety tasks with artificial constraints on hazard and scene types, thereby limiting model generalization; and (3) absence of rigorous evaluation protocols to thoroughly assess model capabilities in complex home safety scenarios. To address these challenges, we introduce TSHA (\textbf{T}rustworthy \textbf{S}afety \textbf{H}azards \textbf{A}ssessment), a comprehensive benchmark comprising 81,809 carefully curated training samples drawn from four complementary sources: existing indoor datasets, internet images, AIGC images, and newly captured images. This benchmark set also includes a highly challenging test set with 1707 samples, comprising not only a carefully selected subset from the training distribution but also newly added videos and panoramic images containing multiple safety hazards, used to evaluate the model's robustness in complex safety scenarios. Extensive experiments on 23 popular VLMs demonstrate that current VLMs lack robust capabilities for safety hazard assessment. Importantly, models trained on the TSHA training set not only achieve a significant performance improvement of up to +18.3 points on the TSHA test set but also exhibit enhanced generalizability across other benchmarks, underscoring the substantial contribution and importance of the TSHA benchmark.

AI.tldr: Advances in vision-language models have improved indoor safety hazards assessment, but existing benchmarks have significant limitations. This paper introduces the TSHA benchmark to address those challenges with diverse data sources and rigorous evaluation protocols.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks for safety hazards assessment in indoor environments are constrained by synthetic datasets, oversimplified tasks, and lack comprehensive evaluation protocols.
AI.method: The TSHA benchmark compiles 81,809 diverse training samples from four sources and includes a challenging test set of 1,707 samples with videos and panoramic images, ensuring rigorous assessment of model robustness.
AI.result: Experiments on 23 VLMs show current models struggle with safety hazard assessment; training on TSHA improves performance by up to 18.3 points and enhances generalizability on other benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The TSHA benchmark addresses critical gaps in current safety hazards assessment benchmarks and significantly improves model robustness and generalization capabilities.

title: How much of persistent homology is topology? A quantitative decomposition for spin model phase transitions
summary: Point-cloud persistent homology (PH) -- computing alpha or Rips complexes on spin-position point clouds -- has been widely applied to detect phase transitions in classical spin models since Donato et al. (2016), with subsequent studies attributing the detection to the topological content of the persistence diagram. We ask a simple question that has not been posed: what fraction of the PH signal is genuinely topological? We introduce f_topo, a quantitative decomposition that separates the density-driven and topological contributions to any PH statistic by comparing real spin configurations against density-matched shuffled null models. Across the 2D Ising model (system sizes L = 16-128, ten temperatures) and Potts models (q = 3, 5), we find that H_0 statistics -- total persistence, persistence entropy, feature count -- are 94-100% density-driven (f_topo < 0.07). The density-matched shuffled null detects T_c at the identical location and with comparable peak height as real configurations, showing that density alone is sufficient for phase transition detection. However, H_1 statistics are partially topological: the topological fraction grows with system size as delta(TP_{H_1}) ~ L^{0.53} and follows a finite-size scaling collapse delta(T, L) = L^{0.53} g(tL^{1/nu}) with collapse quality CV = 0.27. The longest persistence bar is strongly topological (f_topo > 1) and scales with the correlation length. A scale-resolved analysis reveals that the topological excess shifts from large-scale to small-scale features as L increases. We propose that the TDA-for-phase-transitions community adopt shuffled null models as standard practice, and that H_1 rather than H_0 statistics be used when genuine topological information is sought.

AI.tldr: The paper studies the contribution of topology in Persistent Homology (PH) analysis for detecting phase transitions in spin models, revealing the dominant role of density-driven signals and proposing standard practices for future research.
AI.motivation: Persistent Homology has been used in detecting phase transitions in spin models, but the specific contribution of topological versus density-driven signals remains unclear.
AI.method: The authors introduced f_topo to separate density-driven and topological contributions, and analyzed the PH statistics across the 2D Ising and Potts models.
AI.result: The study found that H_0 statistics are mostly density-driven, while H_1 statistics exhibit a partial topological contribution, increasing with system size.
AI.conclusion: Density alone can detect phase transitions, but topological contributions exist and should be studied using H_1 statistics. Shuffled null models are recommended for verifying findings.

title: Beyond Ground-Truth: Leveraging Image Quality Priors for Real-World Image Restoration
summary: Real-world image restoration aims to restore high-quality (HQ) images from degraded low-quality (LQ) inputs captured under uncontrolled conditions. Existing methods typically depend on ground-truth (GT) supervision, assuming that GT provides perfect reference quality. However, GT can still contain images with inconsistent perceptual fidelity, causing models to converge to the average quality level of the training data rather than achieving the highest perceptual quality attainable. To address these problems, we propose a novel framework, termed IQPIR, that introduces an Image Quality Prior (IQP)-extracted from pre-trained No-Reference Image Quality Assessment (NR-IQA) models-to guide the restoration process toward perceptually optimal outputs explicitly. Our approach synergistically integrates IQP with a learned codebook prior through three key mechanisms: (1) a quality-conditioned Transformer, where NR-IQA-derived scores serve as conditioning signals to steer the predicted representation toward maximal perceptual quality. This design provides a plug-and-play enhancement compatible with existing restoration architectures without structural modification; and (2) a dual-branch codebook structure, which disentangles common and HQ-specific features, ensuring a comprehensive representation of both generic structural information and quality-sensitive attributes; and (3) a discrete representation-based quality optimization strategy, which mitigates over-optimization effects commonly observed in continuous latent spaces. Extensive experiments on real-world image restoration demonstrate that our method not only surpasses cutting-edge methods but also serves as a generalizable quality-guided enhancement strategy for existing methods. The code is available.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces IQPIR, a framework for real-world image restoration that utilizes a novel Image Quality Prior (IQP) to explicitly guide outputs toward optimal perceptual quality.
AI.motivation: Existing image restoration methods rely on ground truth (GT), which might not yield perceptually optimal results as they converge to the average quality of the training data.
AI.method: The approach integrates IQP obtained from No-Reference Image Quality Assessment (NR-IQA) models with a learned codebook prior using a quality-conditioned Transformer, dual-branch codebook structure, and a discrete representation-based optimization strategy.
AI.result: IQPIR surpasses state-of-the-art methods in real-world image restoration and enhances quality in existing restoration architectures without any structural changes.
AI.conclusion: IQPIR is a general and effective framework for perceptual quality improvement in image restoration, providing better results than prior works while being adaptable to existing methods.

title: From Skeletons to Semantics: Design and Deployment of a Hybrid Edge-Based Action Detection System for Public Safety
comment: Preprint version of a manuscript currently under review at IEEE Access
summary: Public spaces such as transport hubs, city centres, and event venues require timely and reliable detection of potentially violent behaviour to support public safety. While automated video analysis has made significant progress, practical deployment remains constrained by latency, privacy, and resource limitations, particularly under edge-computing conditions. This paper presents the design and demonstrator-based deployment of a hybrid edge-based action detection system that combines skeleton-based motion analysis with vision-language models for semantic scene interpretation. Skeleton-based processing enables continuous, privacy-aware monitoring with low computational overhead, while vision-language models provide contextual understanding and zero-shot reasoning capabilities for complex and previously unseen situations. Rather than proposing new recognition models, the contribution focuses on a system-level comparison of both paradigms under realistic edge constraints. The system is implemented on a GPU-enabled edge device and evaluated with respect to latency, resource usage, and operational trade-offs using a demonstrator-based setup. The results highlight the complementary strengths and limitations of motioncentric and semantic approaches and motivate a hybrid architecture that selectively augments fast skeletonbased detection with higher-level semantic reasoning. The presented system provides a practical foundation for privacy-aware, real-time video analysis in public safety applications.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a hybrid edge-based system for detecting violent behavior in public spaces using skeleton-based motion analysis and vision-language models.
AI.motivation: Ensuring public safety in spaces like transport hubs demands reliable detection of violent behavior while addressing latency, privacy, and resource challenges under edge-computing conditions.
AI.method: The proposed system combines skeleton-based motion analysis for continuous, computationally light monitoring with vision-language models for semantic reasoning. It is tested on a GPU-enabled edge device.
AI.result: The comparative evaluation showcased the complementary strengths of motion-focused and semantic methods, leading to a hybrid architecture that balances privacy, speed, and resource efficiency.
AI.conclusion: A hybrid approach was found to enhance real-time, privacy-aware video analysis for public safety, suggesting practical advantages for deployment in edge environments.

title: MAPLE: Multi-Path Adaptive Propagation with Level-Aware Embeddings for Hierarchical Multi-Label Image Classification
comment: REO: Advances in Representation Learning for Earth Observation, accepted workshow paper at EurIPS
summary: Hierarchical multi-label classification (HMLC) is essential for modeling structured label dependencies in remote sensing. Yet existing approaches struggle in multi-path settings, where images may activate multiple taxonomic branches, leading to underuse of hierarchical information. We propose MAPLE (Multi-Path Adaptive Propagation with Level-Aware Embeddings), a framework that integrates (i) hierarchical semantic initialization from graph-aware textual descriptions, (ii) graph-based structure encoding via graph convolutional networks (GCNs), and (iii) adaptive multi-modal fusion that dynamically balances semantic priors and visual evidence. An adaptive level-aware objective automatically selects appropriate losses per hierarchy level. Evaluations on CORINE-aligned remote sensing datasets (AID, DFC-15, and MLRSNet) show consistent improvements of up to +42% in few-shot regimes while adding only 2.6% parameter overhead, demonstrating that MAPLE effectively and efficiently models hierarchical semantics for Earth observation (EO).

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MAPLE, a framework for hierarchical multi-label classification in remote sensing, achieving significant accuracy improvements while minimally increasing model complexity.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for remote sensing hierarchical multi-label classification struggle with multi-path scenarios where multiple taxonomic branches are active. Addressing this problem is vital for accurately modeling structured label dependencies.
AI.method: MAPLE incorporates hierarchical semantic initialization via graph-aware textual descriptions, graph-based structure encoding using GCNs, adaptive multi-modal fusion, and a level-aware objective that adjusts losses dynamically.
AI.result: The MAPLE framework achieves up to a 42% improvement in accuracy in few-shot scenarios on remote sensing datasets while only increasing parameter load by 2.6%.
AI.conclusion: MAPLE provides an efficient and effective solution for modeling hierarchical label semantics, outperforming existing approaches in remote sensing applications.

title: Predictor-Based Output-Feedback Control of Linear Systems with Time-Varying Input and Measurement Delays via Neural-Approximated Prediction Horizons
summary: Due to simplicity and strong stability guarantees, predictor feedback methods have stood as a popular approach for time delay systems since the 1950s. For time-varying delays, however, implementation requires computing a prediction horizon defined by the inverse of the delay function, which is rarely available in closed form and must be approximated. In this work, we formulate the inverse delay mapping as an operator learning problem and study predictor feedback under approximation of the prediction horizon. We propose two approaches: (i) a numerical method based on time integration of an equivalent ODE, and (ii) a data-driven method using neural operators to learn the inverse mapping. We show that both approaches achieve arbitrary approximation accuracy over compact sets, with complementary trade-offs in computational cost and scalability. Building on these approximations, we then develop an output-feedback predictor design for systems with delays in both the input and the measurement. We prove that the resulting closed-loop system is globally exponentially stable when the prediction horizon is approximated with sufficiently small error. Lastly, numerical experiments validate the proposed methods and illustrate their trade-offs between accuracy and computational efficiency.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses challenges in predictor feedback methods for time delay systems with time-varying delays by proposing numerical and data-driven approaches to approximate the inverse delay mapping, validating their performance trade-offs, and proving stability of the resulting control system.
AI.motivation: Time delay systems with time-varying delays require efficient techniques to approximate the prediction horizon, which is crucial for implementing predictor feedback methods but is rarely available in closed form.
AI.method: The paper formulates the inverse delay mapping as an operator learning problem and proposes two approaches: (i) a numerical method based on time integration of an equivalent ODE, and (ii) a data-driven method utilizing neural operators to learn the inverse mapping.
AI.result: Both proposed methods achieve arbitrary approximation accuracy over compact sets, with complementary trade-offs between computational cost and scalability. Stability of the proposed predictor feedback system is proven under sufficiently accurate approximation.
AI.conclusion: The methods provide practical tools to address implementation challenges in time delay systems, ensuring stability with flexibility in accuracy versus computational efficiency trade-offs.

title: Multi-Feature Fusion Approach for Generative AI Images Detection
comment: This work has been submitted to IEEE Transactions for possible publication
summary: The rapid evolution of Generative AI (GenAI) models has led to synthetic images of unprecedented realism, challenging traditional methods for distinguishing them from natural photographs. While existing detectors often rely on single-feature spaces, such as statistical regularities, semantic embeddings, or texture patterns, these approaches tend to lack robustness when confronted with diverse and evolving generative models. In this work, we investigate and systematically evaluate a multi-feature fusion framework that combines complementary cues from three distinct spaces: (1) Mean Subtracted Contrast Normalized (MSCN) features capturing low-level statistical deviations; (2) CLIP embeddings encoding high-level semantic coherence; and (3) Multi-scale Local Binary Patterns (MLBP) characterizing mid-level texture anomalies. Through extensive experiments on four benchmark datasets covering a wide range of generative models, we show that individual feature spaces exhibit significant performance variability across different generators. Crucially, the fusion of all three representations yields superior and more consistent performance, particularly in a challenging mixed-model scenario. Compared to state-of-the-art methods, the proposed framework yields consistently improved performance across all evaluated datasets. Overall, this work highlights the importance of hybrid representations for robust GenAI image detection and provides a principled framework for integrating complementary visual cues.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a multi-feature fusion framework to improve the robustness of AI-generated image detection by integrating low-level statistical, mid-level texture, and high-level semantic features, achieving superior performance across diverse generative models.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the limitations of traditional detectors in reliably distinguishing AI-generated images from natural photographs, especially as generative models evolve and exhibit greater realism.
AI.method: The authors combine three complementary feature spaces: MSCN features for low-level statistical analysis, CLIP embeddings for high-level semantic coherence, and MLBP for mid-level texture patterns, into a hybrid detection framework.
AI.result: The framework achieves superior and more consistent detection performance across four benchmark datasets, particularly excelling in mixed-model scenarios and surpassing state-of-the-art detectors.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that hybrid representations integrating diverse visual features are essential for robust detection of generative AI images, providing a principled approach to improve detection accuracy across evolving models.

title: CivicShield: A Cross-Domain Defense-in-Depth Framework for Securing Government-Facing AI Chatbots Against Multi-Turn Adversarial Attacks
summary: LLM-based chatbots in government services face critical security gaps. Multi-turn adversarial attacks achieve over 90% success against current defenses, and single-layer guardrails are bypassed with similar rates. We present CivicShield, a cross-domain defense-in-depth framework for government-facing AI chatbots. Drawing on network security, formal verification, biological immune systems, aviation safety, and zero-trust cryptography, CivicShield introduces seven defense layers: (1) zero-trust foundation with capability-based access control, (2) perimeter input validation, (3) semantic firewall with intent classification, (4) conversation state machine with safety invariants, (5) behavioral anomaly detection, (6) multi-model consensus verification, and (7) graduated human-in-the-loop escalation. We present a formal threat model covering 8 multi-turn attack families, map the framework to NIST SP 800-53 controls across 14 families, and evaluate using ablation analysis. Theoretical analysis shows layered defenses reduce attack probability by 1-2 orders of magnitude versus single-layer approaches. Simulation against 1,436 scenarios including HarmBench (416), JailbreakBench (200), and XSTest (450) achieves 72.9% combined detection [69.5-76.0% CI] with 2.9% effective false positive rate after graduated response, while maintaining 100% detection of multi-turn crescendo and slow-drift attacks. The honest drop on real benchmarks versus author-generated scenarios (71.2% vs 76.7% on HarmBench, 47.0% vs 70.0% on JailbreakBench) validates independent evaluation importance. CivicShield addresses an open gap at the intersection of AI safety, government compliance, and practical deployment.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CivicShield, a 7-layer defense system for securing government AI chatbots against advanced multi-turn adversarial attacks, showing significant improvements over single-layer defenses.
AI.motivation: Address critical security vulnerabilities in government-facing AI chatbots, which are not effectively tackled by existing defenses.
AI.method: Proposes a novel 7-layer defense framework called CivicShield that combines concepts from areas such as network security and zero-trust cryptography, applies formalized threat modeling, and evaluates the defenses using simulations and benchmarks.
AI.result: CivicShield reduces attack probability by 1-2 orders of magnitude compared to existing methods, achieves detection rates of over 72.9% combined across various benchmark scenarios, and retains 100% detection capability against certain advanced attacks with minimal false positives.
AI.conclusion: CivicShield provides a robust, multi-layered, and compliance-focused solution for protecting government AI chatbots, addressing specific gaps in AI safety and deployment practicality.

title: Sampling-Horizon Neural Operator Predictors for Nonlinear Control under Delayed Inputs
summary: Modern control systems frequently operate under input delays and sampled state measurements. A common delay-compensation strategy is predictor feedback; however, practical implementations require solving an implicit ODE online, resulting in intractable computational cost. Moreover, predictor formulations typically assume continuously available state measurements, whereas in practice measurements may be sampled, irregular, or temporarily missing due to hardware faults. In this work, we develop two neural-operator predictor-feedback designs for nonlinear systems with delayed inputs and sampled measurements. In the first design, we introduce a sampling-horizon prediction operator that maps the current measurement and input history to the predicted state trajectory over the next sampling interval. In the second design, the neural operator approximates only the delay-compensating predictor, which is then composed with the closed-loop flow between measurements. The first approach requires uniform sampling but yields residual bounds that scale directly with the operator approximation error. In contrast, the second accommodates non-uniform, but bounded sampling schedules at the cost of amplified approximation error, revealing a practical tradeoff between sampling flexibility and approximation sensitivity for the control engineer. For both schemes, we establish semi-global practical stability with explicit neural operator error-dependent bounds. Numerical experiments on a 6-link nonlinear robotic manipulator demonstrate accurate tracking and substantial computational speedup of 25$\times$ over a baseline approach.

AI.tldr: This study introduces two neural-operator-based predictor-feedback designs for systems with input delays and sampled measurements, achieving notable computational efficiency and stability guarantees.
AI.motivation: To address computational inefficiencies and practical challenges of input delay compensation in control systems with sampled or irregular state measurements.
AI.method: Two neural-operator-based designs are proposed: (1) a sampling-horizon prediction operator for uniform sampling, and (2) a delay-compensating predictor accommodating non-uniform sampling with some trade-offs.
AI.result: Both approaches ensure semi-global practical stability with explicit error bounds, showing a 25x computational speedup in numerical experiments with a nonlinear robotic manipulator.
AI.conclusion: Neural operator designs balance sampling flexibility, approximation sensitivity, and computational efficiency, offering promising control solutions for delayed systems with sampled measurements.

title: SceneTeract: Agentic Functional Affordances and VLM Grounding in 3D Scenes
summary: Embodied AI depends on interactive 3D environments that support meaningful activities for diverse users, yet assessing their functional affordances remains a core challenge. We introduce SceneTeract, a framework that verifies 3D scene functionality under agent-specific constraints. Our core contribution is a grounded verification engine that couples high-level semantic reasoning with low-level geometric checks. SceneTeract decomposes complex activities into sequences of atomic actions and validates each step against accessibility requirements (e.g., reachability, clearance, and navigability) conditioned on an embodied agent profile, using explicit physical and geometric simulations. We deploy SceneTeract to perform an in-depth evaluation of (i) synthetic indoor environments, uncovering frequent functional failures that prevent basic interactions, and (ii) the ability of frontier Vision-Language Models (VLMs) to reason about and predict functional affordances, revealing systematic mismatches between semantic confidence and physical feasibility even for the strongest current models. Finally, we leverage SceneTeract as a reward engine for VLM post-training, enabling scalable distillation of geometric constraints into reasoning models. We release the SceneTeract verification suite and data to bridge perception and physical reality in embodied 3D scene understanding.

AI.tldr: SceneTeract is a framework that evaluates 3D scenes' functionality and their compatibility with embodied agents by combining semantic reasoning with geometric checks.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address challenges in assessing functional affordances in interactive 3D environments essential for embodied AI applications, ensuring these environments support meaningful activities for diverse users.
AI.method: SceneTeract decomposes complex activities into atomic actions and validates each step using simulations conditioned on agent-specific constraints, employing both semantic reasoning and geometric checks.
AI.result: The framework uncovers frequent functional failures in synthetic indoor environments, exposes gaps in Vision-Language Models' reasoning about functional affordances, and enhances model performance through post-training using SceneTeract.
AI.conclusion: SceneTeract bridges the gap between perception and physical reality in 3D scene understanding, serving as a crucial tool for improving embodied AI systems and their interaction with virtual environments.

title: Adaptive Delayed-Update Cyclic Algorithm for Variational Inequalities
summary: Cyclic block coordinate methods are a fundamental class of first-order algorithms, widely used in practice for their simplicity and strong empirical performance. Yet, their theoretical behavior remains challenging to explain, and setting their step sizes -- beyond classical coordinate descent for minimization -- typically requires careful tuning or line-search machinery. In this work, we develop $\texttt{ADUCA}$ (Adaptive Delayed-Update Cyclic Algorithm), a cyclic algorithm addressing a broad class of Minty variational inequalities with monotone Lipschitz operators. $\texttt{ADUCA}$ is parameter-free: it requires no global or block-wise Lipschitz constants and uses no per-epoch line search, except at initialization. A key feature of the algorithm is using operator information delayed by a full cycle, which makes the algorithm compatible with parallel and distributed implementations, and attractive due to weakened synchronization requirements across blocks. We prove that $\texttt{ADUCA}$ attains (near) optimal global oracle complexity as a function of target error $ε>0,$ scaling with $1/ε$ for monotone operators, or with $\log^2(1/ε)$ for operators that are strongly monotone.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ADUCA, a cyclic algorithm that improves theoretical understanding and step-size issues in cyclic block coordinate methods for Minty variational inequalities.
AI.motivation: To address theoretical challenges and step-size tuning problems in cyclic block coordinate methods for monotone Lipschitz operators.
AI.method: The authors propose the Adaptive Delayed-Update Cyclic Algorithm (ADUCA), which is parameter-free, uses delayed operator information for compatibility with parallel implementations, and avoids per-epoch line searches.
AI.result: ADUCA achieves (near) optimal global oracle complexity, scaling at $1/ε$ for monotone operators and $\log^2(1/ε)$ for strongly monotone operators.
AI.conclusion: ADUCA offers a robust and efficient solution for solving Minty variational inequalities with weakened synchronization, eliminating the need for extensive parameter tuning.

title: AutoFormBench: Benchmark Dataset for Automating Form Understanding
summary: Automated processing of structured documents such as government forms, healthcare records, and enterprise invoices remains a persistent challenge due to the high degree of layout variability encountered in real-world settings. This paper introduces AutoFormBench, a benchmark dataset of 407 annotated real-world forms spanning government, healthcare, and enterprise domains, designed to train and evaluate form element detection models. We present a systematic comparison of classical OpenCV approaches and four YOLO architectures (YOLOv8, YOLOv11, YOLOv26-s, and YOLOv26-l) for localizing and classifying fillable form elements. specifically checkboxes, input lines, and text boxes across diverse PDF document types. YOLOv11 demonstrates consistently superior performance in both F1 score and Jaccard accuracy across all element classes and tolerance levels.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces AutoFormBench, a dataset aimed at improving the detection of form elements like checkboxes and input lines in varied documents. YOLOv11 outperforms other compared approaches.
AI.motivation: The difficulty in automated processing of forms arises due to the high variability in real-world document layouts, necessitating robust solutions for accurate detection and classification of fillable form elements.
AI.method: The authors developed AutoFormBench, a dataset comprising annotated real-world forms, and systematically evaluated classical OpenCV techniques along with four YOLO architectures for form element detection and classification.
AI.result: Among the studied approaches, YOLOv11 consistently achieved the highest F1 score and Jaccard accuracy for detecting and classifying form elements like checkboxes, input lines, and text boxes.
AI.conclusion: YOLOv11 is a superior model for the task of form element localization and classification, and AutoFormBench serves as a valuable benchmark for improving automated form processing systems.

title: Toward Generalizable Whole Brain Representations with High-Resolution Light-Sheet Data
summary: Unprecedented visual details of biological structures are being revealed by subcellular-resolution whole-brain 3D microscopy data, enabled by recent advances in intact tissue processing and light-sheet fluorescence microscopy (LSFM). These volumetric data offer rich morphological and spatial cellular information, however, the lack of scalable data processing and analysis methods tailored to these petabyte-scale data poses a substantial challenge for accurate interpretation. Further, existing models for visual tasks such as object detection and classification struggle to generalize to this type of data. To accelerate the development of suitable methods and foundational models, we present CANVAS, a comprehensive set of high-resolution whole mouse brain LSFM benchmark data, encompassing six neuronal and immune cell-type markers, along with cell annotations and a leaderboard. We also demonstrate challenges in generalization of baseline models built on existing architectures, especially due to the heterogeneity in cellular morphology across phenotypes and anatomical locations in the brain. To the best of our knowledge, CANVAS is the first and largest LSFM benchmark that captures intact mouse brain tissue at subcellular level, and includes extensive annotations of cells throughout the brain.

AI.tldr: CANVAS provides a benchmark dataset with high-resolution LSFM data of mouse brains to address challenges in processing and analyzing large-scale subcellular microscopy data.
AI.motivation: Advances in tissue processing and LSFM enable unprecedented imaging details of biological structures, but scalable data analysis methods are lacking, especially for massive datasets.
AI.method: They present CANVAS, a benchmark dataset with annotated LSFM images of whole mouse brains along with performance leaderboards for evaluating models.
AI.result: CANVAS provides subcellular level imaging of mouse brains with annotations, highlighting the challenges for existing general models due to cellular variation.
AI.conclusion: CANVAS pioneers as a foundational LSFM dataset, promoting the development of methods for analyzing large-scale subcellular biological data in mouse brains.

title: Less Is More? Selective Visual Attention to High-Importance Regions for Multimodal Radiology Summarization
summary: Automated radiology report summarization aims to distill verbose findings into concise clinical impressions, but existing multimodal models often struggle with visual noise and fail to meaningfully improve over strong text-only baselines in the FINDINGS $\to$ IMPRESSION transformation. We challenge two prevailing assumptions: (1) that more visual input is always better, and (2) that multimodal models add limited value when findings already contain rich image-derived detail. Through controlled ablations on MIMIC-CXR benchmark, we show that selectively focusing on pathology-relevant visual patches rather than full images yields substantially better performance. We introduce ViTAS, Visual-Text Attention Summarizer, a multi-stage pipeline that combines ensemble-guided MedSAM2 lung segmentation, bidirectional cross-attention for multi-view fusion, Shapley-guided adaptive patch clustering, and hierarchical visual tokenization feeding a ViT. ViTAS achieves SOTA results with 29.25% BLEU-4 and 69.83% ROUGE-L, improved factual alignment in qualitative analysis, and the highest expert-rated human evaluation scores. Our findings demonstrate that less but more relevant visual input is not only sufficient but superior for multimodal radiology summarization.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces ViTAS, a method for automated radiology report summarization focused on pathology-relevant visual patches rather than full images, yielding SOTA results.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses challenges in multimodal summarization of radiology reports, where existing models struggle with visual noise and fail to surpass text-only baselines.
AI.method: ViTAS employs selective focus on pathology-relevant visual patches using MedSAM2 segmentation, multi-view fusion via cross-attention, patch clustering guided by Shapley values, and hierarchical visual tokenization integrated with a Vision Transformer.
AI.result: ViTAS achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) summarization performance with metrics like 29.25% BLEU-4, 69.83% ROUGE-L, improved factual alignment, and high expert-rated evaluation scores.
AI.conclusion: The findings highlight that selectively focusing on relevant visual data is more effective than using full images for multimodal radiology summarization.

title: Diffusion-Based Feature Denoising with NNMF for Robust handwritten digit multi-class classification
summary: This work presents a robust multi-class classification framework for handwritten digits that combines diffusion-driven feature denoising with a hybrid feature representation. Inspired by our previous work on brain tumor classification, the proposed approach operates in a feature space to improve the robustness to noise and adversarial attacks. First, the input images are converted into tight, interpretable exemplification using Nonnegative Matrix Factorization (NNMF). In parallel, special deep features are extracted using a computational neural network (CNN). These integral features are combined into a united hybrid representation. To improve robustness, a step diffusion operation is used in the feature space by gradually adding Gaussian noise. A feature denoiser network is trained to reverse this operation and rebuild clean representations from tilted inputs. The courteous features are then applied for multi-class classification. The suggested method is evaluated in both baseline and adversarial settings using AutoAttack. The experimental outcome present that the diffusion-based hybrid model is both effective and robust, the CNN baseline models outperforming while maintain powerful classification performance. These results explain the activity of feature-level diffusion defense for reliable multi-class handwritten digit classification.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a robust classification system for handwritten digits, combining denoising and hybrid feature representation techniques to counter noise and adversarial attacks.
AI.motivation: To improve the reliability and robustness of classification systems for handwritten digits, especially against noise and adversarial threats.
AI.method: The approach involves creating hybrid representations combining NNMF and CNN features. A diffusion operation adds Gaussian noise, and a neural network denoises features for robust classification.
AI.result: Experimental findings show the proposed method is robust and effective in both baseline and adversarial tests, outperforming CNN baselines.
AI.conclusion: Feature-level diffusion defense enhances multi-class handwritten digit classification, offering a robust and powerful framework.

title: Evaluating a Data-Driven Redesign Process for Intelligent Tutoring Systems
comment: Accepted as short paper to the 27th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence in Education (AIED 2026)
summary: Past research has defined a general process for the data-driven redesign of educational technologies and has shown that in carefully-selected instances, this process can help make systems more effective. In the current work, we test the generality of the approach by applying it to four units of a middle-school mathematics intelligent tutoring system that were selected not based on suitability for redesign, as in previous work, but on topic. We tested whether the redesigned system was more effective than the original in a classroom study with 123 students. Although the learning gains did not differ between the conditions, students who used the Redesigned Tutor had more productive time-on-task, a larger number of skills practiced, and greater total knowledge mastery. The findings highlight the promise of data-driven redesign even when applied to instructional units *not* selected as likely to yield improvement, as evidence of the generality and wide applicability of the method.

AI.tldr: The study tested the generality of a data-driven redesign approach for educational technologies, finding promise in its application, even outside of topics specifically chosen for likely improvement.
AI.motivation: To determine whether a data-driven redesign process for educational technologies can be widely applicable and effective beyond preselected suitable instances.
AI.method: The authors applied a redesign process to four units of a middle-school mathematics tutoring system, tested its effectiveness through a classroom study with 123 students, and measured outcomes including learning gains, time-on-task, skills practiced, and knowledge mastery.
AI.result: While learning gains did not differ between conditions, the Redesigned Tutor led to more productive engagement, higher skills practiced, and greater knowledge mastery among students.
AI.conclusion: The findings support the general applicability of the data-driven redesign methodology, demonstrating its potential across varying instructional contexts.

title: Training deep learning based dynamic MR image reconstruction using synthetic fractals
summary: Purpose: To investigate whether synthetically generated fractal data can be used to train deep learning (DL) models for dynamic MRI reconstruction, thereby avoiding the privacy, licensing, and availability limitations associated with cardiac MR training datasets. Methods: A training dataset was generated using quaternion Julia fractals to produce 2D+time images. Multi-coil MRI acquisition was simulated to generate paired fully sampled and radially undersampled k-space data. A 3D UNet deep artefact suppression model was trained using these fractal data (F-DL) and compared with an identical model trained on cardiac MRI data (CMR-DL). Both models were evaluated on prospectively acquired radial real-time cardiac MRI from 10 patients. Reconstructions were compared against compressed sensing(CS) and low-rank deep image prior (LR-DIP). All reconstrctuions were ranked for image quality, while ventricular volumes and ejection fraction were compared with reference breath-hold cine MRI. Results: There was no significant difference in qualitative ranking between F-DL and CMR-DL (p=0.9), while both outperformed CS and LR-DIP (p<0.001). Ventricular volumes and function derived from F-DL were similar to CMR-DL, showing no significant bias and accptable limits of agreement compared to reference cine imaging. However, LR-DIP had a signifcant bias (p=0.016) and wider lmits of agreement. Conclusion: DL models trained using synthetic fractal data can reconstruct real-time cardiac MRI with image quality and clinical measurements comparable to models trained on true cardiac MRI data. Fractal training data provide an open, scalable alternative to clinical datasets and may enable development of more generalisable DL reconstruction models for dynamic MRI.

AI.tldr: This paper explores training deep learning models for cardiac MRI reconstruction using synthetically generated fractal data instead of real cardiac MRI, showing comparable performance between both approaches.
AI.motivation: Limitations in privacy, licensing, and availability of cardiac MRI datasets make it difficult to build robust deep learning models. The authors aim to explore fractal data as an alternative training dataset.
AI.method: Quaternion Julia fractals were used to create synthetic dynamic images simulating multi-coil MRI acquisition. These datasets trained a 3D UNet deep learning model, whose results were compared to both a model trained on real cardiac MRI and other reconstruction methods (CS, LR-DIP).
AI.result: Deep learning models trained on fractal data (F-DL) performed comparably to those trained on cardiac MRI data (CMR-DL), outperforming CS and LR-DIP. Ventricular volumes and function derived from F-DL also showed no significant bias.
AI.conclusion: Using synthetic fractal data for DL training is feasible and provides comparable image quality and clinical measurements to real cardiac MRI training data, offering an accessible and scalable alternative.

title: Abstraction in Style
summary: Artistic styles often embed abstraction beyond surface appearance, involving deliberate reinterpretation of structure rather than mere changes in texture or color. Conventional style transfer methods typically preserve the input geometry and therefore struggle to capture this deeper abstraction behavior, especially for illustrative and nonphotorealistic styles. In this work, we introduce Abstraction in Style (AiS), a generative framework that separates structural abstraction from visual stylization. Given a target image and a small set of style exemplars, AiS first derives an intermediate abstraction proxy that reinterprets the target's structure in accordance with the abstraction logic exhibited by the style. The proxy captures semantic structure while relaxing geometric fidelity, enabling subsequent stylization to operate on an abstracted representation rather than the original image. In a second stage, the abstraction proxy is rendered to produce the final stylized output, preserving visual coherence with the reference style. Both stages are implemented using a shared image space analogy, enabling transformations to be learned from visual exemplars without explicit geometric supervision. By decoupling abstraction from appearance and treating abstraction as an explicit, transferable process, AiS supports a wider range of stylistic transformations, improves controllability, and enables more expressive stylization.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a framework called Abstraction in Style (AiS) designed to separate structural abstraction from visual stylization in style transfer tasks.
AI.motivation: Conventional style transfer methods lack the ability to deeply abstract structural elements and semantic reinterpretations often seen in nonphotorealistic styles.
AI.method: The framework includes two stages: first, creating an intermediate abstraction proxy based on target structure and style exemplars; second, rendering the proxy to produce stylized output, using shared image space analogy without geometric supervision.
AI.result: AiS supports broader stylistic transformations by separating abstraction from visual appearance, improves stylization controllability, and handles nonphotorealistic abstractions effectively.
AI.conclusion: By treating abstraction as an explicit, transferable process, AiS enhances stylistic expressiveness and expands the potential of generative style transfer systems.

title: Real-Time Surrogate Modeling for Fast Transient Prediction in Inverter-Based Microgrids Using CNN and LightGBM
summary: Real-time monitoring of inverter-based microgrids is essential for stability, fault response, and operational decision-making. However, electromagnetic transient (EMT) simulations, required to capture fast inverter dynamics, are computationally intensive and unsuitable for real-time applications. This paper presents a data-driven surrogate modeling framework for fast prediction of microgrid behavior using convolutional neural networks (CNN) and Light Gradient Boosting Machine (LightGBM). The models are trained on a high-fidelity EMT digital twin dataset of a microgrid with ten distributed generators under eleven operating and disturbance scenarios, including faults, noise, and communication delays. A sliding-window method is applied to predict important system variables, including voltage magnitude, frequency, total active power, and voltage dip. The results show that model performance changes depending on the type of variable being predicted. The CNN demonstrates high accuracy for time-dependent signals such as voltage, with an $R^2$ value of 0.84, whereas LightGBM shows better performance for structured and disturbance-related variables, achieving an $R^2$ of 0.999 for frequency and 0.75 for voltage dip. A combined CNN+LightGBM model delivers stable performance across all variables. Beyond accuracy, the surrogate models also provide major improvements in computational efficiency. LightGBM achieves more than $1000\times$ speedup and runs faster than real time, while the hybrid model achieves over $500\times$ speedup with near real-time performance. These findings show that data-driven surrogate models can effectively represent microgrid dynamics. They also support real-time and faster-than-real-time predictions. As a result, they are well-suited for applications such as monitoring, fault analysis, and control in inverter-based power systems.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a machine learning-based framework using CNN and LightGBM to monitor inverter-based microgrid dynamics in real time, delivering high accuracy and faster-than-real-time computational speeds.
AI.motivation: The motivation of the study is to address the computational limitations of electromagnetic transient (EMT) simulations in inverter-based microgrids, which are critical for stability and operational control.
AI.method: The study develops surrogate models using CNN and LightGBM, trained on high-fidelity EMT simulation data from microgrids under various operating conditions. A sliding-window method is utilized for predicting key system variables.
AI.result: The experiments show CNN excelling in predicting time-dependent signals, LightGBM performing better for disturbance-related variables, and a hybrid CNN+LightGBM model providing balanced accuracy across variables. The models also achieve significant computational speedups, with LightGBM running over 1000× faster than real time.
AI.conclusion: The data-driven surrogate modeling can computationally replace traditional EMT simulations for real-time microgrid monitoring and control, offering both high accuracy and substantial improvements in efficiency.

title: End-to-End Image Compression with Segmentation Guided Dual Coding for Wind Turbines
summary: Transferring large volumes of high-resolution images during wind turbine inspections introduces a bottleneck in assessing and detecting severe defects. Efficient coding must preserve high fidelity in blade regions while aggressively compressing the background. In this work, we propose an end-to-end deep learning framework that jointly performs segmentation and dual-mode (lossy and lossless) compression. The segmentation module accurately identifies the blade region, after which our region-of-interest (ROI) compressor encodes it at superior quality compared to the rest of the image. Unlike conventional ROI schemes that merely allocate more bits to salient areas, our framework integrates: (i) a robust segmentation network (BU-Netv2+P) with a CRF-regularized loss for precise blade localization, (ii) a hyperprior-based autoencoder optimized for lossy compression, and (iii) an extended bits-back coder with hierarchical models for fully lossless blade reconstruction. Furthermore, our ROI framework removes the sequential dependency in bits-back coding by reusing background-coded bits, enabling parallelized and efficient dual-mode compression. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first fully integrated learning-based ROI codec combining segmentation, lossy, and lossless compression, ensuring that subsequent defect detection is not compromised. Experiments on a large-scale wind turbine dataset demonstrate superior compression performance and efficiency, offering a practical solution for automated inspections.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a deep learning framework for wind turbine inspection images that integrates segmentation and dual-mode compression to efficiently preserve blade fidelity while aggressively compressing backgrounds.
AI.motivation: Wind turbine inspections of high-resolution images require efficient processing to preserve blade details and overcome bottlenecks caused by large-scale data transfers.
AI.method: The paper proposes a framework using BU-Netv2+P for segmentation, a hyperprior-based autoencoder for lossy compression, extended bits-back coding for lossless compression, and a parallelized approach to ROI coding.
AI.result: Experiments show that the framework achieves superior compression and efficiency on a wind turbine dataset without compromising defect detection accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The proposed integrated ROI codec is a practical advancement for automated wind turbine inspections, enabling effective compression while preserving critical defect assessment data.

title: "I Just Need GPT to Refine My Prompts": Rethinking Onboarding and Help-Seeking with Generative 3D Modeling Tools
summary: Learning to use feature-rich software is a persistent challenge, but generative AI tools promise to lower this barrier by replacing complex navigation with natural language prompts. We investigated how people approach prompt-based tools for 3D modeling in an observational study with 26 participants (14 casuals, 12 professionals). Consistent with earlier work, participants skipped tutorials and manuals, relying on trial and error. What differed in the generative AI context was how and why they sought support: the prompt box became the entry point for learning, collapsing onboarding into immediate action, while some casual users turned to external LLMs for prompts. Professionals used 3D expertise to refine iterations and critically evaluated outputs, often discarding models that did not meet their standards, whereas casual users settled for "good enough." We contribute empirical insights into how generative AI reshapes help-seeking, highlighting new practices of onboarding, recursive AI-for-AI support, and shifting expertise in interpreting outputs.

AI.tldr: Generative AI tools facilitate natural language interactions for feature-rich software, changing learning approaches and help-seeking behaviors for 3D modeling.
AI.motivation: To explore how people adapt to generative AI-driven prompt-based tools for 3D modeling, particularly in terms of learning and expertise application.
AI.method: An observational study with 26 participants (14 casual users, 12 professionals) focusing on their interactions and learning behaviors with generative AI tools while modeling.
AI.result: Participants used trial and error while relying on AI prompts for actions and support. Professionals applied expertise to refine outputs, while casual users often accepted subpar results.
AI.conclusion: Generative AI reshapes onboarding and help-seeking behaviors, introduces recursive AI support, and alters professional and casual user standards in interpreting outputs.

title: Gloria: Consistent Character Video Generation via Content Anchors
comment: Accepted by CVPR2026 Main, project: https://yyvhang.github.io/Gloria_Page/
summary: Digital characters are central to modern media, yet generating character videos with long-duration, consistent multi-view appearance and expressive identity remains challenging. Existing approaches either provide insufficient context to preserve identity or leverage non-character-centric information as the memory, leading to suboptimal consistency. Recognizing that character video generation inherently resembles an outside-looking-in scenario.
